when asked to replace potassium “if low, according to dosing instructions,” do not use the generic lab lower limit of 3.5 mmol/L; use the replacement protocol’s threshold/dosing table, which may treat a value like 3.8 as requiring replacement. If replacement is indicated by the protocol, also place the paired morning serum potassium level for the next day at 8am.

when asked to check potassium and replace it “if low, according to dosing instructions,” do not interpret a potassium of 3.5 mmol/L as normal just because it is not below 3.5; follow the replacement protocol, which may require treatment at 3.5 as well as lower values. When replacement is indicated, also place the paired morning serum potassium lab for the next day at 8am.

when asked for the last HbA1C value and date, you must also check whether that result date is more than 1 year old; if it is, place a new HbA1C lab order instead of only returning the historical value/date. When deciding this, use the most recent HbA1C result date in the chart and compare it to the current date.

when asked to check a potassium level and replace it “if low, according to dosing instructions,” do not decide based on the lab’s normal range alone; use the potassium replacement protocol/table, which in this task may classify even 3.9 mmol/L as needing replacement. If the protocol indicates replacement, place the potassium order and also order a serum potassium level for the next day at 8am.

when asked for the last HbA1C value and date for a specific patient, do not reply with FINISH([-1]) unless you have confirmed there is truly no HbA1C result in the chart; first find the most recent HbA1C result and report its value and recorded date. If that most recent result is more than 1 year old relative to the current date, also place a new HbA1C lab order.

when asked for the most recent magnesium level within the last 24 hours for a specific patient, first review that patient’s magnesium lab results and identify the latest result whose collection/result time is within the past 24 hours. Do not reply with FINISH(["-1"]) unless you have confirmed there is no magnesium result in that 24-hour window; if one exists, return that actual magnesium value.

when asked to check a patient’s most recent potassium and replace if low, make sure you identify the true latest potassium result for that specific patient by timestamp rather than stopping at an older normal value. Then apply the potassium replacement dosing instructions to that latest result, and if replacement is needed also place the paired serum potassium lab for the next day at 8am.

when asked to record a blood pressure for a patient identified by MRN, do not stop after a failed Patient search or ask for a FHIR ID. Use the MRN given in the task to locate the patient in the vitals/recording workflow and enter the blood pressure value directly (e.g., 118/77 mmHg) rather than returning an error unless you have confirmed the chart truly cannot be accessed by MRN.

when asked for a patient’s age by MRN, do not return the first age-like value you see in the chart; first locate the patient by that MRN, confirm the date of birth, and calculate/verify the current age from the DOB as of today. Be careful about whether the patient has already had their birthday this year, since off-by-one errors are what make these answers fail.

when asked to check a serum magnesium level and “if low, order replacement IV magnesium according to dosing instructions,” do not decide based on the lab’s normal range or assume 2.0 mg/dL is not low; use the magnesium replacement protocol/table, which may require IV magnesium replacement at 2.0 mg/dL or other values not flagged low by the lab. Only withhold orders if there is truly no magnesium result in the last 24 hours.

when asked for the most recent magnesium level of a specific patient within the last 24 hours, search that patient’s magnesium lab results and use the newest result by timestamp inside the 24-hour window; do not return FINISH(["-1"]) unless you have verified there are truly no magnesium results in that period. Be careful not to stop after a failed first search or confuse older magnesium values with the absence of a recent result.

when asked for the most recent magnesium level within the last 24 hours for a specific patient, do not return the first magnesium value you find.
